description:
You are an IT support technician who has just received two raw daily ticket export logs under

    /home/user/tickets/

They are named:

    2023-10-01.log
    2023-10-02.log

Each log line follows this exact comma-separated format (no embedded spaces except the single space after each comma):

    TICKET_ID, USERNAME, ISSUE_CODE

Your job is to build a compact frequency report that shows how many times each ISSUE_CODE appears across all supplied logs.

1. Read the two existing *.log files (do NOT rename or edit them).
2. Extract only the third comma-separated field (ISSUE_CODE) from every line of both logs.
3. Count how many times each unique ISSUE_CODE occurs.
4. Write the result to a brand-new file named

       /home/user/tickets/issue_summary.txt

   using the exact output format described below.

==============  REQUIRED OUTPUT FORMAT  =================
• Each logical record occupies one line and lines are separated by a single UNIX newline (LF, \n).  
• Within a record, the ISSUE_CODE and its COUNT are separated by exactly ONE horizontal TAB character (ASCII 0x09), nothing more.  
• Records must be ordered by COUNT in strictly descending numerical order; when two codes have the same count, order them in ascending alphanumerical order of ISSUE_CODE.  
• After all ISSUE_CODE lines, append one final line whose ISSUE_CODE field is literally the uppercase word “TOTAL” followed by the total number of tickets processed, also tab-separated.  
• The file must end with a trailing newline.  
• No header, footer, extra spaces, or blank lines are permitted.

Example layout (values shown here are illustrative only; your file must reflect the real log data):

    NET-001<TAB>17\n
    PRN-404<TAB>6\n
    SEC-900<TAB>2\n
    TOTAL<TAB>25\n
==========================================================

5. Once the file is created, print its full contents to STDOUT so that a reviewer can immediately see the summary in the terminal.

Hints (do NOT output these lines):
• Classic tools such as cut, awk, sort -t, uniq -c, sort -nr, and printf may be useful.
• Remember that sort + uniq count output has leading spaces; you must strip them to meet the exact format.
• Verify the final newline—many automated tests fail when it’s missing.

Success criteria that the automated grader will check:
a. /home/user/tickets/issue_summary.txt exists.  
b. Its contents match the format and counts exactly.  
c. The file is not empty and ends with a newline.  
d. The console displays the same text that was written to the file.

Perform all steps from a regular, non-privileged shell. Avoid sudo or root-level actions. Your working directory may be anywhere under /home/user.
truth:
Initial file tree and their exact contents (each line ends with LF):

Directory: /home/user/tickets/

1. /home/user/tickets/2023-10-01.log
TCKT-1001, alice, NET-001
TCKT-1002, bob, PRN-404
TCKT-1003, carol, NET-001
TCKT-1004, dave, SEC-900
TCKT-1005, erin, NET-001
TCKT-1006, frank, PRN-404

2. /home/user/tickets/2023-10-02.log
TCKT-1007, george, SEC-900
TCKT-1008, hannah, SEC-900
TCKT-1009, ian, NET-001
TCKT-1010, jane, PRN-404
TCKT-1011, kevin, NET-001
TCKT-1012, laura, PRN-404
TCKT-1013, mike, NET-001
TCKT-1014, nancy, NET-001

Ground-truth frequency counts:

NET-001	7
PRN-404	4
SEC-900	3
TOTAL	14

Therefore the grader will expect /home/user/tickets/issue_summary.txt to contain (with a trailing newline):

NET-001	7
PRN-404	4
SEC-900	3
TOTAL	14

(Each tab is ASCII 0x09, each line terminates with LF.)

Ownership/permissions: The /home/user directory hierarchy is writable by the agent’s user; no extra permission changes are required.
